title: Where innovation embraces tradition – Celebrating 100 years of Patritti
summary:
A suburban street in the middle of Adelaide’s Dover Gardens is perhaps the last place many would expect to find a cellar door, let alone a full-blown winemaking operation. Driving through the gates of Patritti is like entering an alternative universe, where modern signage stands proudly beside decades-old Grenache vines adorning the entry. Once you’re inside, the 21st-century houses across the road feel a world away, and as the antique bricks come into view, one truth rings clear – Patritti was here first. As the 100% family-owned urban winery celebrates 100 years in operation, journalist Kyra Trussell was invited to explore what a century of winemaking means to third-generation Patritti family member and senior winemaker James Mungall.

The post Where innovation embraces tradition – Celebrating 100 years of Patritti appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
By Kyra Trussell

A suburban street in the middle of Adelaide’s Dover Gardens is perhaps the last place many would expect to find a cellar door, let alone a full-blown winemaking operation. Driving through the gates of Patritti is like entering an alternative universe, where modern signage stands proudly beside decades-old Grenache vines adorning the entry. Once you’re inside, the 21st-century houses across the road feel a world away, and as the antique bricks come into view, one truth rings clear – Patritti was here first. As the 100% family-owned urban winery celebrates 100 years in operation, Grapegrower & Winemaker was invited to explore what a century of winemaking means to third-generation Patritti family member and senior winemaker James Mungall.

 

Celebrations have already begun for Patritti’s 100th anniversary, with a members’ grape-picking event hosted at the Marion Vineyard back in late February.

Mungall shared that it had been raining when the event was due to start at 7:30am, so he was worried about the turnout. However, he was pleasantly surprised to see people show up in their raincoats excited and ready to lend a hand.

In the end, the group helped pick half a tonne of Shiraz grapes, which was commemorated with lunch and music.

Wine club members picking event for the 100th urban vineyard vintage.

Mungall excitedly called this year’s anniversary a “huge milestone”.

“It’s 101 years since Giovanni Patritti arrived in Australia, 100 years since he started making wine on this site, and we’ve made wine every year here for the last 100 years.

“We’ve always been fully operational, which we still remain to this day. So, we’re still processing and going the whole way through to glass and container packing and export on the one site,” he explained.

Patritti’s founder, Giovanni Patritti, was among the first Italian immigrants to land in South Australia. The man was Mungall’s grandfather, and he made wine for other European immigrants all through the 1930s and 1940s.

Giovanni and his eldest son Peter Patritti. Image supplied.

Mungall himself has worked at Patritti full-time officially since 2001, yet he grew up immersed in the family business, recalling that he “never really thought [of] anything other than wine” for his future career.

“Basically, it’s all I’ve ever done…I just kind of jumped into it and loved it all the different facets of it, all the way from grapegrowing, winemaking, production and sales,” he shared.

There’s been a lot of changes over the century of Patritti’s existence, from surviving World War II scrutiny as “enemy aliens” to losing vineyards. Mungall recalled the most trying times he could remember.

“I’ve seen a couple of cycles. At the moment, we’re in a really bad oversupply situation. But then also, as a child, I remember in the mid-1980s or late 1980s burning Grenache vines at our vineyard in McLaren Vale.

“I’ve seen the cycles in both directions, seen good times where there’s been an investment in machinery, and obviously times like now, where everyone’s trying hard to tread water and survive the next upswing.”

The winery used to have 10 hectares of on-site vineyards; however, those were lost to the government following a compulsory acquisition in the late 1950s.

“Post World War Two, there was a massive housing boom in Adelaide, and then, around the mid-1950s, the houses had caught up to us here. Then three or four years later, there was lots of kids in the area from the new houses, and the government didn’t actually leave any land for education, so the last of our vineyard area was acquired by the government at the time to build a school,” he detailed.

Taken mid 1950’s – Patritti was a place for the local Italian community to come together. Image supplied.

The winery has changed owners recently, although Mungall is adamant that the winery has managed to retain the same family values it has always been known for.

“There has been a change of ownership, but under a different family now. The original Patritti family has had a long connection with the new family that’s taken over the business. They’re quite passionate about the brand, about the history, and they’ve had a really long connection, both families together, so nothing’s really changed. There’s still the same values there to drive the business forward.”

 

Strength in diversity

As for what Patritti’s biggest strength is, Mungall flagged the winery’s diversity.

“We’ve always been quite diverse, we’re multi-regional now. We do have our own vineyard in McLaren Vale, but we also source fruit from the urban vineyard. It’s quite unique and quite remarkable that two hectares of vineyards have survived in the local area, so we actually make a range of wines from the urban vineyards. They date right back to the early 1900s, so we make wine from those vineyards, which is really, really unique.”

A behind the scenes look at Patritti’s winemaking operations.

Mungall believes this uniqueness, coupled with the winery’s ‘genuine’ nature, is what will allow Patritti to continue to persevere, even through today’s ever-changing consumer trends.

“I think we are genuine. As I mentioned, we’re grapegrowing, not on this site, but grapegrowing all the way through to winemaking, through to production. This site really celebrates a bit of Adelaide winemaking history. We’re sitting now in the original building that’s made with handmade bricks by the first and second generations. So, it’s a real story built on wine. Lots of stories and lots of history, and it’s all tangible, and it still exists to this day.”

He is not wrong – tradition and heritage are very much embraced at the Patritti site. The history is not stuffed in a box, shoved under the bed for when it’s convenient to dust off, rather, it’s proudly displayed, and one might get the sense that any new developments were built around the old.

Each memory hung upon the walls is smiled at fondly, and the past family members are remembered and spoken about with respect.

A humble understanding of early beginnings is carefully woven with the ambitions of tomorrow.

The exact mould used by the first and second generation Patritti family to handmake bricks for the winery.

As for those future innovations, Mungall disclosed that there are a few developments in the works.

“We’re looking at different juice products now, so it’s a bit of diversity in the no-alc stuff. It’s ever evolving, and we’re looking for new trends. With the new ownership structure, they’ve got ideas and markets, so hopefully they can not only give us direction on what the consumer wants, but make it happen.”

Mungall exclusively revealed to the Grapegrower & Winemaker that a new Moscato from Patritti is set to release within the next few months. The wine will be made from vines at Oaklands Estate which were planted back in 1924.

Inside the Patritti barrel room.

For any wine enthusiasts wanting to celebrate the 100th anniversary of Patritti, the winery hosts tastings every day of the week and has live music nights with food trucks every Friday to Sunday night.

“Towards the end of September and through to Christmas, we’re really going to celebrate the 100 years of continuous winemaking on this site here in Dover Gardens. So, a lot to look forward to!” Mungall exclaimed.

title: Sydney to host pop-up clinic for tasting treasured wines without opening them
summary:
On 27 August, Sydney will host the world’s first ‘Coravin Aged Wine Clinic’, allowing wine enthusiasts to taste their most prized vintages “without ever removing the cork or interrupting the aging process”. Held at Grappa, The Rocks, organisers say the clinic will offer the “rare opportunity” for ticket holders to conduct a ‘health-check’ of the bottle they have always wanted to open, guided by Master of Wine Andrea Pritzker. Each bookable 15-minute consultation invites wine lovers and collectors to bring one special bottle from home to be expertly assessed. Andrea Pritzker MW will use Coravin’s ‘Timeless By-the-Glass System’, fitted with the ‘Coravin Vintage Needle’, which has been “specifically engineered to pass safely through old and fragile corks without damaging them”, to extract two small tasting serves. As the wine is poured, the system replaces it with Argon, a neutral gas widely used in the winemaking process. Upon removal, the cork “naturally reseals itself, allowing the remaining wine to continue its ageing journey completely undisturbed”.

The post Sydney to host pop-up clinic for tasting treasured wines without opening them appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The clinic will allow collectors and wine lovers to taste their most prized wines without popping the cork. Image supplied

On 27 August, Sydney will host the world’s first ‘Coravin Aged Wine Clinic’, allowing wine enthusiasts to taste their most prized vintages “without ever removing the cork or interrupting the aging process”.

Held at Grappa, The Rocks, organisers say the clinic will offer the “rare opportunity” for ticket holders to conduct a ‘health-check’ of the bottle they have always wanted to open, guided by Master of Wine Andrea Pritzker.

Each bookable 15-minute consultation invites wine lovers and collectors to bring one special bottle from home to be expertly assessed. Andrea Pritzker MW will use Coravin’s ‘Timeless By-the-Glass System’, fitted with the ‘Coravin Vintage Needle’, which has been “specifically engineered to pass safely through old and fragile corks without damaging them”, to extract two small tasting serves.

As the wine is poured, the system replaces it with Argon, a neutral gas widely used in the winemaking process. Upon removal, the cork “naturally reseals itself, allowing the remaining wine to continue its ageing journey completely undisturbed”.

Organisers emphasised that wines will be assessed purely for their evolution and ideal ageing window, rather than monetary value. Following the tasting, Pritzker will provide custom tasting notes “capturing a snapshot of the wine’s current development”. The bottle will then be stamped with a Coravin seal, the tasting date and a recommendation on when to revisit the wine.

“An aged bottle of wine is a time capsule, but historically, the only way to know if it was at its peak was to open it, which is a rather final commitment,” said Coravin founder and inventor Greg Lambrecht.

“I invented Coravin because I wanted the ability to check in on that journey. I wanted to taste how a bottle was evolving at year five, year ten, and year fifteen. Our Aged Wine Clinic gives Sydney’s wine lovers that same ability to taste their rarest bottles, knowing they can come back to them again when the time is right,” he concluded.

Andrea Pritzker, one of only 26 Masters of Wine in Australia, brings “decades of elite fine wine expertise” to the clinic.

“Whether you want to bring the most impressive bottle you own, a wild card wine you are curious about, or a bottle with deep sentimental value, I can’t wait to taste it with you.

“With a glass in hand, we can discuss the history and trajectory of the wine together,” she said.

Beyond the private assessments, the pop-up features the ‘Coravin Tasting Lounge’, where attendees can “enjoy canapés, connect with like-minded wine enthusiasts and taste rare vintage wines from esteemed producers like Hickinbotham, Yangarra and Torbreck”.

The tasting lounge will also include special bottles from Grappa’s reserve cellar. Lounge access is included for Aged Wine Clinic ticket holders but can also be purchased separately for those wanting to taste rare wines without bringing their own bottle.

The pop-up clinic will kick-off a month-long celebration of vintage wine across Sydney. Throughout September, select venues will pour a limited-edition list of vintage wines 10 years or older. Wine enthusiasts can discover participating locations here.

Tickets are on sale for RRP $199 plus booking fee. Tickets to the Coravin Tasting Lounge can also be purchased separately for $90 plus booking fee. More information on the wine clinic can be found here.

title: University study shows nutrient loss in vineyards occurs beyond fruit harvest
summary:
New research from Washington State University could help grapegrowers improve nutrient use efficiency and develop a more accurate fertiliser budget. The findings, published in the American Journal of Enology and Viticulture, show that “leaf fall, dormant cane removal and environmental conditions can significantly impact nutrient loss in grapevines”. “The study highlights the importance of taking a more holistic view of vineyard nutrient management,” said lead author Nataliya Shcherbatyuk, a research associate in WSU’s Department of Horticulture. “While growers routinely account for nutrients removed through harvested fruit, there are other pathways that receive much less attention despite their potential contribution to annual nutrient losses. This research is about understanding how vineyard systems function and how we can use that knowledge to make more informed management decisions.”

The post University study shows nutrient loss in vineyards occurs beyond fruit harvest appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
New research from Washington State University could help grapegrowers improve nutrient use efficiency and develop a more accurate fertiliser budget.

The findings, published in the American Journal of Enology and Viticulture, show that “leaf fall, dormant cane removal and environmental conditions can significantly impact nutrient loss in grapevines”.

“The study highlights the importance of taking a more holistic view of vineyard nutrient management,” said lead author Nataliya Shcherbatyuk, a research associate in WSU’s Department of Horticulture.

“While growers routinely account for nutrients removed through harvested fruit, there are other pathways that receive much less attention despite their potential contribution to annual nutrient losses. This research is about understanding how vineyard systems function and how we can use that knowledge to make more informed management decisions.”

Part of the USDA-funded ‘High Resolution Vineyard Nutrient Management Project’, the study analysed nutrient loss data on Syrah, Sauvignon Blanc and Chardonnay wine grape cultivars as well as Concord juice grapes. The samples came from irrigated vineyards in Eastern Washington.

The scientists found that nutrient loss is “heavily dependent on crop yield”, and nutrients such as potassium and nitrogen are lost in the largest quantities through harvested grapes. Meanwhile, leaf fall accounted for “significant calcium and nitrogen losses”.

“In some cases, nutrient losses through leaves were much larger than many growers might expect,” Shcherbatyuk said.

The study also showed how changing weather patterns can make nutrient management difficult for growers, claiming that drought conditions can negatively affect a vine’s nutrient uptake, while strong winds often carry grape leaves out of the vineyard and deposit them where the nutrients are inaccessible. The study found that increasingly warmer autumns often mean the plants stay active later, only to lose their leaves to a sudden frost before the nutrient recycling process is complete.

“At the end of a growing season, the dying leaves will recycle nutrients back into the vine’s roots, where they are stored until the next season,” said Markus Keller, project director and Chateau Ste. Michelle distinguished professor in WSU’s Department of Viticulture and Enology.

“With an early frost, that doesn’t happen. If early frosts occur on a routine basis, the plants will eventually become nutrient deficient because those nutrients aren’t available at bud break the following season. Growers must then compensate with fertiliser.”

Similarly, a warm spring encourages earlier bud burst, but freezing temperatures could mean the plants have to start over.

“Both situations are problematic, because you’re essentially running the vine out of nutrients,” Keller said.

The study found that volatile weather can also impact crop yield, canopy growth and vine biomass, which all influence annual nutrient removal. Researchers say that a better understanding of this relationship can help growers make more informed decisions.

“This research is an excellent example of our university’s land-grant mission in action,” said Raj Khosla, Cashup Davis Family endowed dean of WSU’s College of Agricultural, Human and Natural Resource Sciences.

“Our scientists are working on important research that directly impacts the health of vineyards, allowing grapegrowers to manage their vineyards as efficiently as possible.”

Many vineyards in the western US are fertilised via drip irrigation systems that are impossible to customise for precision, or variable-rate, nutrient applications across heterogeneous vineyards, Keller said.

WSU scientists have partnered with Oregon State University researchers to install a single vine drip irrigation prototype at the WSU Prosser research vineyard. Developed by an OSU team led by Chad Higgins, the mechanism could allow for more precise fertiliser application while accounting for the many variables that contribute to nutrient loss.

Other next steps include studying nutrient loss in different cultivars, vineyard sites, climates and management sites, added Shcherbatyuk. The nutrient management project has already developed decision tools, such as leaf nutrient sensors, and maps that help growers improve their fertiliser timing while reducing unnecessary inputs.

“I hope this work helps stimulate additional discussion and research around vineyard nutrient cycling,” Shcherbatyuk said.

“There is still much to learn about how nutrients move through vineyard ecosystems, and a better understanding of those processes can benefit growers, researchers and the industry as a whole.”

title: Entries open for Melbourne Royal Wine Awards 2026
summary:
The Melbourne Royal Wine Awards is now accepting entries for 2026, inviting winemakers from across the country to benchmark their wines alongside the nation’s best. Judging for the awards will take place at the Melbourne Showgrounds from 19 to 23 October, with entries assessed through a “rigorous blind-tasting process by expert judging panels comprising leading winemakers, wine professionals and industry associates”. Wines will be grouped regionally to enable like-for-like comparisons and assessed using the internationally recognised 100-point scoring system. Organisers explained that trophy winners will undergo multiple rounds of evaluation, including re-tasting and ranking through the Borda Count method, “ensuring only the most exceptional wines receive Australia's most respected wine accolades”.

The post Entries open for Melbourne Royal Wine Awards 2026 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The awards feature a suite of “highly regarded trophies”. Image supplied

The Melbourne Royal Wine Awards is now accepting entries for 2026, inviting winemakers from across the country to benchmark their wines alongside the nation’s best.

Judging for the awards will take place at the Melbourne Showgrounds from 19 to 23 October, with entries assessed through a “rigorous blind-tasting process by expert judging panels comprising leading winemakers, wine professionals and industry associates”.

Wines will be grouped regionally to enable like-for-like comparisons and assessed using the internationally recognised 100-point scoring system. Organisers explained that trophy winners will undergo multiple rounds of evaluation, including re-tasting and ranking through the Borda Count method, “ensuring only the most exceptional wines receive Australia’s most respected wine accolades”.

The awards feature a suite of “highly regarded trophies”, including the Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy (affectionately known as ‘The Jimmy’) which celebrates the legacy of beloved Carlton wine merchant and wine bar owner Jimmy Watson.

Other “coveted” trophies include the François de Castella Trophy for Best Young White Wine, the Champion Victorian Winery Trophy, the Douglas Seabrook Single Vineyard Trophy and the Kym Ludvigsen Trophy for Viticultural Excellence, which recognises the grower or viticulture team behind the winning single vineyard wine.

The Melbourne Royal Wine Awards also celebrate some of the industry’s most influential figures through dedicated honours, including the Dr Tony Jordan Trophy for Best Sparkling, the James Halliday Trophy for Best Pinot Noir, the Trevor Mast Trophy for Best Shiraz and the Bill Chambers Trophy for Best Fortified Wine.

In total, more than 20 major trophies will be awarded, including Best Sparkling, Best Riesling, Best Chardonnay, Best Shiraz, Best Pinot Noir, Best Cabernet Sauvignon, Best Grenache, Best Organic or Biodynamic Wine and Best Design, alongside state-based trophies “recognising excellence in key varietals”.

Kirrily Waldhorn, Melbourne Royal senior manager food & beverage awards, said the awards provide a vital platform for Aussie winemakers to benchmark their wines against the very best.

“Beyond the opportunity to compete for the industry’s most prestigious honours, including the iconic Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy, the awards celebrate excellence across every region and style, while recognising the innovation and craftsmanship driving Australian winemaking forward,” Waldhorn said.

Chair of judges, Marcus Satchell, is looking forward to seeing what this year brings for the awards.

“The standard of entries continues to be exceptionally high year on year, reflecting the depth and diversity of Australian winemaking and so I’m looking forward to seeing what 2026 brings. Our judging process is rigorous and considered, ensuring every wine is assessed fairly and consistently through blind tasting to identify true excellence across each category,” Satchell said.

While organisers say the 2026 awards submission stays relatively the same as previous years, notable updates include streamlined judging for Best Single Varietal White, Best Single Varietal Red and Best Red Blend, with classes no longer split by varietal and vintage, but by varietal only. The event hosts said this change will provide greater submission opportunities for exhibitors.

Meanwhile, the Design Award criteria has been refined following industry feedback to ensure “a simpler and more relevant assessment process with exhibitors also having the opportunity to provide additional product information to support judging”.

Organisers said the Melbourne Royal continues to invest in the future of Australian winemaking through the ‘Melbourne Royal Wine Awards Professional Development Scholarship’. Awarded annually to the winemaker behind the Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy-winning wine, or an employee of that winery, the scholarship supports “professional development opportunities that help recipients further their careers and contribution to the Australian wine industry”.

Established in 1883 with only 13 entries, the awards now attract more than 2,300 entries annually.

content:
Grenache festival ‘Lost in the FoG’ will return to Alkina Wine Estate in the Barossa Valley this August for its fifth year as well as make its inaugural Sydney debut in the same month.

Lost in the FoG is set to bring “more than 20 of Australia’s most acclaimed Grenache producers for an immersive weekend celebrating one of the country’s most exciting red grape varieties”.

Organisers say the festival offers guests “the opportunity to spend an entire day exploring the remarkable diversity of Australian Grenache while meeting the growers and winemakers shaping its future”.

Alkina Wine Estate winemaker Amelia Nolan said Grenache is a variety that continues to surprise and intrigue.

“Every producer brings something different to the conversation, and that sense of discovery is what makes Lost in the FoG so special.

“It is a rare opportunity for guests to spend time with the people behind the wines, ask questions, learn about different approaches and experience the incredible diversity of Australian Grenache all in one place,” Nolan said.

Lost in the FoG will share its passion for Grenache with the Eastern Seaboard for the first time, making its Sydney debut on 1 August at The Wine Bar at The International.

This event poster. Image supplied

Running from 2 pm to 5 pm in Sydney and 11 am to 4 pm on both days in Barossa, event hosts shared that Lost in the FoG “is a rare opportunity to compare styles, discover new favourites and explore why Grenache has become one of Australia’s most talked-about wine varieties”.

title: Independent ag machinery repairers to discuss ‘right to repair’ reforms
summary:
A roundtable bringing together independent agricultural machinery repairers will be held this week to discuss the proposed expansion of Australia’s ‘right to repair’ law in regard to farm machinery. The Australian Automotive Aftermarket Association (AAAA), Australia's national industry association representing independent mechanical repairers, will convene a 'National Agricultural Machinery Repairers Roundtable' on Wednesday. The roundtable will bring together a small group of independent agricultural machinery repairers from across Australia for a practical online discussion. Treasury officials responsible for the current right to repair review will attend the roundtable as observers to hear directly from independent repairers.

content:
A roundtable bringing together independent agricultural machinery repairers will be held this week to discuss the proposed expansion of Australia’s ‘right to repair’ law in regard to farm machinery.

The Australian Automotive Aftermarket Association (AAAA), Australia’s national industry association representing independent mechanical repairers, will convene a ‘National Agricultural Machinery Repairers Roundtable’ on Wednesday.

The roundtable will bring together a small group of independent agricultural machinery repairers from across Australia for a practical online discussion. Treasury officials responsible for the current right to repair review will attend the roundtable as observers to hear directly from independent repairers.

The federal government is actively considering whether the Motor Vehicle Information Scheme (MVIS) should be expanded beyond passenger vehicles to include agricultural machinery.

The AAAA says that for the review to be informed by real-world experience, government needs to hear directly from the independent repair businesses that keep Australia’s farms operating. Independent agricultural machinery repairers provide an essential service across regional and rural Australia, yet their experience is often absent from formal policy discussions, according to the association.

“Repairers are busy. They’re regional. They’re focused on keeping farmers working, not participating in Canberra policy processes,” said Lesley Yates, director of government relations and advocacy at the AAAA.

“Unless government hears directly from the businesses doing this work every day, there is a real risk that any future right to repair framework will be designed around assumptions rather than real-world experience.”

Rather than formal presentations, the discussion will focus on practical examples of the barriers repairers encounter when servicing modern agricultural machinery.

Participants will discuss the machinery they service, the work they are prevented from undertaking because of restricted access to information, software and diagnostic functions, how improved access could expand services for local farming communities, the practical issues government should address if agricultural machinery is brought within Australia’s right to repair framework, and the vital role independent repairers play in keeping farmers and regional communities operating.

The discussion is expected to include real-world examples involving diagnostic software, scan tools, security codes, software updates, calibrations, telematics, GPS-enabled equipment, connected machinery platforms, parts access, parts pairing, dealer-only functions and the impacts these restrictions have on farmers through increased downtime, travel costs and reduced service options.

“Independent repairers already support Australia’s agricultural sector every day,” Yates said.

“Where they have the information and tools they need, they keep machinery operating quickly and close to the farm. Where access is restricted, farmers can face unnecessary delays, higher costs and longer travel distances simply because independent repairers are prevented from completing work that they are fully capable of undertaking.”

“The people designing these laws need to understand what is actually happening in workshops and on farms across regional Australia. The practical experience shared through this roundtable will help ensure any future right to repair framework works for farmers as well as repairers.”

content:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further.

Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry.

 

Read our recent roundup here:

 

Responding to Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone

“Doug Bowen would get a fair few nominations for best bloke in the wine game. As Classic Wine Merchants in the early 80’s, we were Doug and Joy’s agents in SA and to put it simply, Bowen wine was easy to sell. Behind Dougie’s dry-as (and often funny-as) exterior was a smart and simple strategy to make very good, often best, Coonawarra wine and at modest prices. Not a bad approach to ensure you get to 50 vintages in this challenging caper. Well done!”

  • David Ridge

 

Responding to Seeing ghosts

“Impressive article and can’t disagree with any of Edward’s analysis and conclusions. We’re in a parlous state as an industry and the duopoly is a major part of that, not that they treat any of their suppliers any better.

“From a lived experience, Delatite sold twice as much wine through Dan’s when it had 4 stores in Melbourne than we did five years after it had gone national. We now only sell a small volume of our Deadman’s Hill Gewurz through them, and I don’t care whether they sell it or not.”

  • David Ritchie
